title: Multiple attack modes
principles: A weapon with multiple normalized attack modes must be evaluated mode by mode before a whole-weapon conclusion is produced.
The first normalized mode must not automatically be treated as the only practical mode when other modes contain distinct triggers, components, or mechanics.
Statistics from separate modes must not be combined into one imaginary attack.
A secondary mode may refine or change the weapon's role when its supplied pattern is materially different from the primary mode.
A mode that appears to be inherited, duplicated, fallback, or auxiliary should be treated cautiously unless its structure or description confirms practical relevance.
Whole-weapon conclusions should identify the dominant mode and describe meaningful secondary behavior separately.
Improvement priorities should favor parameters shared by relevant modes unless the requested analysis explicitly targets one mode.

single_mode: The supplied weapon contains one normalized attack mode.
multiple_similar_modes: Several modes exist, but their supplied behavior is substantially similar.
multiple_distinct_modes: The weapon contains materially different attack modes.
The primary job may depend on which mode is emphasized.
ambiguous_modes: Some modes lack enough labels or mechanics to establish their practical role.
Use cautious wording and preserve uncertainty.

exceptions: Do not merge damage values across modes.
Do not assume an unlabeled mode is an alternate fire.
Do not assume every normalized mode is player-selectable.
Do not let a secondary mode overwrite the primary mode without explicit evidence.
